title: Unit 26 Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass: Active & Passive Participles (Οι Μετοχές)

script.text: Welcome... to the Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass for Unit 26: Participles. These are the 'Verbal Adjectives'. Words like 'running', 'broken', or 'tired'. In Greek, they come in two flavors: Active and Passive. I'm William, and I'm joined by Athina. Athina... why are active participles so 'stable'?
The Active Participles are wonderful because they are indeclinable. They never change their ending! They always end in
Indeclinable... that’s music to my ears. So if I want to say 'running', I take the verb
they came running. It stays exactly the same, whether it's one person or many.
That’s perfect for describing 'how' an action is done. But what about the other kind? The Passive Participles. These are the ones that describe a state, like 'tired'.
Ah, these are more 'traditional'. They end in
Tired, written, opened. And because they act like adjectives, they MUST match the noun in gender and case.
Correct. They behave exactly like the regular adjectives we learned way back in Unit 6.
Pro Tip: If you want to describe 'while doing something', use
If you want to describe the 'result' of something, use
It’s a spelling rule! If the stress is on the 'o', we use omikron:
If the stress is earlier, we use omega:
But the sound is exactly the same!
It’s all about where the beat falls. This concludes Unit 26. You now know how to add movement and state to your Greek. Excellent work.
Μπράβο! Τα λέμε στο επόμενο μάθημα. Γεια σας!
